turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the court this morning in case 23-217 E.M.D. Sales v. Carrera.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938 requires employers to pay their employees a minimum wage and overtime compensation, but the Act exempts many categories of employees from those minimum wage and overtime compensation requirements. The dispute here is a narrow one. It concerns the standard of proof that an employer must satisfy to show that an employee is exempt. Should courts apply the usual civil standard of proof per ponderance of evidence or does a more demanding standard such as clear and convincing evidence apply? In 1938 when Congress enacted the Fair Labor Standards Act, the established default standard proof in American Civil Litigation was the pre-ponderance of the evidence standard. Since then the pre-ponderance of the evidence standard has remained the default standard for proof in American Civil Litigation. That default makes sense. The pre-ponderance standard allows both parties in the mind ran civil case to share the risk of their in roughly equal fashion. In civil litigation the court has deviated from the pre-ponderance standard and three main circumstances. First, courts must apply a heightened standard of proof if a statute establishes one. For example, various provisions of the U.S. Code including some involving labor and employment designate clear and convincing evidence as the applicable standard of proof. Second, courts likewise must apply a heightened standard of proof when the Constitution requires one. For example, the court is mandated a clear and convincing evident standard in certain First Amendment cases. Third, under this court's precedence, a heightened standard of proof may be appropriate in certain other uncommon cases such as those that arise when the government seeks to take unusual course of action as we phrased it, action more dramatic than entering an award of money damages or other conventional relief against an individual. For example, the court has held that the government must satisfy a clear and convincing evidence standard in order to take away a person's citizenship. Importantly, the court has not otherwise used a heightened standard in civil manners. Here as the employees acknowledge the Fair Labor Standards Act does not specify a standard of proof for the Act's exemptions nor does this case implicate any constitutional rights that might implicate a heightened standard, and this is not a case where the government otherwise seeks to take unusual course of action against an individual. Therefore, applying our precedence from the default standard, we hold that the pre-ponderance of the evidence standard applies when an employer seeks to show that an employee is exempt from the minimum wage and overtime pay provisions of the Fair Labor Standards Act. We reverse the judgement of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit and remand the case for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The opinion of the court is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch has filed a concurring opinion which Justice Thomas has joined.
